result:
I.  
The first light is always blue. Not the postcard-blue of mid-afternoon sky or the painter’s cobalt squeezed from a tube, but the bruised, almost ultraviolet glow that leaks under doors and around the edges of blackout curtains while the rest of the house still pretends to be asleep. I have been collecting that color for years without knowing what to do with it—scraping it off the rim of coffee cups at 5:13 a.m., catching it in the condensation on the bathroom mirror, folding it into the fitted sheet that snaps like a sail when I finally give up on sleeping. A hundred soft heists. A private indigo archive. I keep it in an imaginary tobacco tin inherited from a grandfather who never smoked, a tin itself inherited from a poem I read in college and immediately misremembered. Sometimes, when the real world’s saturation dial is twisted too high—orange construction vests, red sale banners, the arterial gush of brake lights on the interstate—I unscrew the invisible lid and let the collected blue sigh out like a gas. It doesn’t fix anything, but it lowers the volume.

II.  
There is a kind of loneliness that behaves like a gas too: odorless, colorless, settling first along the floorboards and then rising until it reaches the level of the eyes. You discover it when you come home carrying three plastic bags of groceries in the crook of each arm, kick the door shut behind you, and realize no one else will ever know the exact weight of those bags unless you tell them. The bananas leave tender green bruises on the underside of your wrist; the carton of milk presses its cold square nose against your elbow. You could post a photograph, I suppose—#groceries, #finallyhome—but the weight itself is non-transferable. The loneliness is not the absence of people; it is the absence of witnesses to the microscopic, the almost-nothing, the way the twist-tie from the kale made a tiny helix against your palm. Whole days go by made of almost-nothings. They accumulate like calcium in pipes, a quiet clogging.

III.  
I once tried to keep a diary of almost-nothings. January 9: The rubber band around the asparagus snapped louder than I expected and I laughed out loud, but the laugh came out like a hiccup. February 2: The barista drew a leaf in the foam that looked more like Australia; I drank Australia. February 27: While waiting for the laundry to finish I counted twenty-three dust motes in the shaft of sunlight and lost count when I wondered if “mote” was related to “moat.” I stopped in April because the notebook began to feel like a mausoleum for moments that deserved to evaporate. Still, I cannot shake the urge to testify. Maybe that is what writing is: an inappropriate memorial, a granite bench bolted to the ground beside a butterfly.

IV.  
When I was ten I believed every adult had an internal dashboard with two dials—one labeled Purpose, the other Pleasure—calibrated by some celestial mechanic at age twenty-one. The idea terrified me because I could already sense my own dials wobbling like cheap radio knobs. At ten, Purpose was “paleontologist” one week, “backup dancer for Janet Jackson” the next; Pleasure swung violently between strawberry Pop-Tarts and the breathless seconds before the elementary-school PA speaker crackled out the afternoon announcements. I assumed that someday the needles would settle and lock, the way my parents’ Oldsmobile radio stopped on 101.5 WTAO (“The best of the ’70s, ’80s, and now!”). Then I would know what I was for. I am thirty-eight and the knobs still spin. They spin in my sleep. They spin while I answer emails, while I watch my neighbor across the alley trim his hedges into vague torpedo shapes. Sometimes I imagine prying the plastic bezel off and disconnecting the wires, letting the dials drop behind the console like fallen coins. Quiet. But I am also afraid that if I open the panel I will find nothing back there—just a cavernous dark and the smell of old ozone.

V.  
Science says the universe is expanding at 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec. I looked up “megaparsec” and immediately forgot the definition, the way I forgot the rules of rugby the instant high-school gym ended. Still, the number 70 lingers like a faint bell. I repeat it while doing ordinary things: 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec while folding towels; 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec while scraping burnt rice off the pot. It is a secular prayer, a reminder that the scaffolding is moving, that nothing is clipped to permanence. Sometimes I picture my living room stretching like taffy—couch and coffee table gliding away from each other at imperceptible speed—until the television remote is a continent away and I must mail myself a postcard to recall what I meant to watch. The expansion is impartial; it tugs at blood vessels and shopping lists and the small scar on my knee shaped like a cat’s eye. One day the gap between the scar and the rest of me will be a canyon. I will stand on one rim, shouting “I got it falling off a Schwinn in 1992!” and only an echo shaped like my older sister’s laugh will answer from the far side.

VI.  
My older sister lives in Brisbane, which is 14 hours ahead of Cincinnati. We have performed this arithmetic so long that time zones feel like family lore. When I call her at 7 p.m. my Tuesday, it is 9 a.m. her Wednesday; we meet on the tightrope of that difference. Sometimes she walks her kids to school while I pour a second glass of wine. Her Wednesdays are always shinier than mine, full of sunscreen and morning energy; my Tuesdays carry the sour dusk of a day already threadbare. We trade weather like baseball cards: she sends photos of king parrots on the clothesline, I send icicles forming on the "NO TURN ON RED" sign. The planet keeps tilting, reckless croupier spinning the roulette ball of sunlight. We attempt to steady it with voice memos, with snapshots of cakes we baked or overcooked. The delay on the line is 600 milliseconds—enough for a micro-flinch when our sentences overlap. That half-second contains an entire childhood: the bunk beds, the Glow-Worm nightlight, the cassette deck singing "500 Miles" until the ribbon warped. We let it pass unremarked, the way astronauts let meteors streak past the capsule rather than reroute for every sparkle.

VII.  
Childhood was a series of unauthorized experiments with physics: How long can you lie on the driveway before the gravel embosses your back like braille? How many times can you spin the lawn sprinkler before the hose kinks and water martyrs itself in a burst of mouth-shaped droplets? We tested centrifugal force by sitting on the tire swing, leaning back until the sky inverted and became a blue lake we might dive into headfirst. My sister’s braids collected grass seed; my knees collected ant bites. We recorded data in mud, in cherry Popsicle drips that looked like forensic maps of small empires. No one told us that the rules we were probing were actually statutes written by tired adults: gravity as unpaid intern to liability, thermodynamics as the reason you can’t have seconds of ice cream if you didn’t finish peas. We thought reality was negotiable. In some folder of the cosmos that version of us is still spinning, arms out, ponytails horizontal, believing that if we just went fast enough we could drill a hole in the air and step through to anywhere.

VIII.  
I have never figured out what to do with the moment after arriving anywhere. Airports, hotel rooms, new cities glowing on the horizon like carnival prizes—there is always an awkward minute when the compass needle keeps quivering even though the map says you have arrived. I stand holding my suitcase handle, waiting for some internal gong to announce, “You are here!” but the gong is on back-order. Instead, the ambient soundtrack of the place rushes in: a taxi driver arguing in Tagalog, a child practicing violin through the thin wall of the Airbnb, the sulfuric burp of radiators older than my parents. I open the closet to hang my shirt and find someone else’s dry-cleaning tag still safety-pinned to the wire hanger: R. CHEN, STARCH LIGHT. I imagine R. Chen waking up in the room after me, finding my forgotten dental floss on the nightstand, and feeling briefly, incorrectly, accompanied. We are serial haunters of each other’s unfinished sentences.

IX.  
 unfinished sentences are the only honest kind. Finish a sentence and you have built a wall; leave it open and the weather wanders in. I draft texts I never send: “I dreamed the moon had a concierge desk and your name was on the—“ Backspace backspace. The cursor blinks like a lighthouse that has run out of shipwrecks. Perhaps this is mercy: to spare the other person the burden of replying to a subjunctive moon. Perhaps it is cowardice dressed as mercy, the same way “I’m fine” is a hazmat suit we zip ourselves into before stepping into the contaminated daylight. I read once that the human heart contains a cluster of neurons—40,000 cells lighting up like a galaxy—so maybe when I press delete those little star-cells dim in sequence, a micro-blackout. If you wired me to a heart monitor delicate enough, would it register the vanishing of a clause? Would the EKG spike at the moment I decide not to say I miss you?

X.  
"Miss" is a small word with a trapdoor in the middle. It can mean the distance between two people who once shared everything. I type “I miss—” and delete. The cursor blinks like a lighthouse that has run out of ships to warn.

X.  
The first time I heard the phrase “ontological shock” I was in a university lecture hall at 8 a.m., my brain a marshmallow left too close to the campfire of adolescence. The professor, a man whose mustache looked like it had been drawn on with a fine-tip pen, said it meant the vertigo of realizing that your way of being in the world is just one of many, that the floor is actually a wall somewhere else. I felt it again years later in a Tokyo convenience store at 3 a.m. when I discovered a plastic-wrapped sandwich labeled “Egg Salad with Emergency Rations Flavor.” The neon lights buzzed like hornets; I stood there holding dinner and breakfast and apocalypse in one chilly palm. Ontological shock tastes of refrigerated egg and iodine. It comes in fluorescent packaging, 298 yen, buy-one-get-one during Golden Week.

XI.  
Convenience stores are liminal spaceports. Walk into any 7-Eleven on earth and you have exited local time. The floor is always the same shade of linoleum asteroid; the beep of the register is the universal heartbeat. I have cried in convenience stores on three continents, always next to the rotating grill of skewered sausages. The sausages glisten like minor planets, eternal and indifferent. Once, in Seoul, a clerk wordlessly handed me a packet of tissues while I wept over a mango-flavored yogurt drink. We did not share a language, but we shared the fluorescent hum, the sausages, the protocol that says: here, wipe your face, keep going. I still have the tissue packet; it is printed with cartoon raccoons wearing bow ties. I carry it like a passport stub from a country I hope never to revisit but appreciate having visited.

XII.  
I once tried to write a love letter entirely in questions. The tactic felt safer, as if interrogative grammar could attenuate risk. “Do you remember the sound of the elevator fan in that hotel in Prague? Was it March or November? Could we measure the distance between two heartbeats in floor numbers?” The letter grew to forty-three questions, then forty-four. I mailed it without a return address, a small cowardice dressed as romance. Weeks later I received a postcard: a photograph of the moon over the Atacama Desert. On the back, one sentence, not a question: “Some things answer themselves by echoing.” I have never been to Chile; perhaps the moon has. Perhaps the echo is still ricocheting through the copper mines, looking for a tin roof to land on.

XIII.  
Echoes are the thrift store of memory: everything secondhand, slightly warped, 75 % off original intent. I find them in the alley behind my apartment where the dumpsters reproduce astounding acoustics. If I stand between them at the right angle and say my childhood phone number, it returns to me in the voice of my mother’s kitchen radio, the one with fake wood paneling and a broken tuning dial that always drifted toward the Spanish station. The echo adds a lisp of static, so the eight becomes ocho, the three becomes tres. I am unreachable at that number now; the line was disconnected sometime between the Y2K scare and my father’s second wedding. Still, I recite it monthly, a prayer to the patron saint of discontinued coordinates.

XIV.  
Discontinued things I miss: the glass bottle of milk delivered to the porch, the paper cap you pressed with your thumb to reveal a dab of cream; the sound of VHS tapes rewinding, a mechanical purr that meant the story was being re-coiled, ready to begin again; the smell of library card catalog drawers, sawdust and ink and the faint perfume of every hand that flipped a card forward; the word “post-modern,” which once sounded like a spaceship and now sounds like a dial-up modem. Nostalgia is a museum built inside the mouth; you wander the galleries tasting dust bunnies and Chiclets, trying to remember which decade they belong to.

XV.  
I keep a list of things that refuse to be nostalgic: automated customer service menus, the smell of bus exhaust in August, passwords that must include a capital and a symbol and the blood of a unicorn. These items will never ripen into comfort; they will stay perpetually raw, like the inner cheek you can’t stop biting. The list is written in the notes app of my phone between the grocery list and a half-remembered dream about tidal waves. Every so often I scroll past it and feel the satisfaction of certitude: some hates deserve to be documented, archived, preserved in brine like medical specimens. Future archaeologists will dig up my phone, scroll with delicate tools, and say: “Here was a woman who loathed the phrase ‘your call is important to us.’” They will award me a posthumous medal forged from discontinued earphone jacks.

XVI.  
Medals are just mirrors wearing uniforms. I learned this at a middle-school awards ceremony where I received a participation ribbon for “Most Improved in Volleyball.” I was allergic to the metallic thread; within minutes my chest erupted in hives shaped like tiny crowns. While the nurse dabbed calamine on my sternum, I understood that validation can be histamine. Since then I have distrusted any accolade that must be pinned to fabric. I prefer applause that disperses immediately, the kind children give when they’ve already turned their backs to run toward the next game. Ephemeral applause is honest; it knows it is leaving.

XVII.  
Leaving is a skill I never mastered. I stand in doorways too long, one foot on the welcome mat, one foot in the future, my tongue stacking goodbyes like a malfunctioning vending machine. The problem is that every threshold looks like a question mark lying on its side, inviting me to drag it upright and punctuate something. Instead I mumble “see you later” to people I will never see again, consigning the sentence to a lifetime of logistical confusion. I once spent twenty minutes saying goodbye to a therapist after our final session; she finally walked me out, patted my shoulder, and said, “It’s okay, endings are just beginnings that have taken off their hat.” I still wonder what kind of hat—top hat, sunbonnet, maybe a fascinator decked with miniature beginnings. I picture them lined up on a shelf in her office, hats looking for heads, heads looking for doors.

XVIII.  
Doors are books with hinges. Open one and you read a new temperature on your skin, a new dialect of creak. I collect doorknobs in a drawer—porcelain, brass, glass like a witch’s crystal ball. When I can’t sleep I hold them and try to intuit which room they once guarded. The oval brass one feels like a hotel in a city where I arrived drenched from a sudden storm; the porcelain one carries a faint odor of rosewater and old tax documents. I rotate them like worry beads, hoping for a transmission, a clue to the topography on the other side of time. Occasionally I imagine screwing one into the wall beside my bed, a door to nowhere, just to have the option. If I ever install it, I will paint “Elsewhere” on the jamb and charge admission: one secret per entry, payable in expired transit tokens.

XIX.  
Tokens are fossils of future archaeology. The subway tokens in my change jar will outlast the system they once animated, just as the brass tokens my grandmother used at the textile mill now lie in my palm like miniature suns. I like to dump them on the table and try to read them like runes. One says: you will be delayed. Another says: transfer available. The smallest, smoothest one says nothing, and that seems the most honest fortune of all. I carry it in my coat pocket as a reminder that silence is a valid currency; sometimes you pay with not-speaking, sometimes you receive.

XX.  
Receiving mail addressed to the previous tenant feels like intercepting a postcard from an alternate universe. I pile the envelopes on the kitchen counter, a small shrine to J. Delgado, who once inhabited these rooms before I did. The electric company wants J. Delgado to know the overdue balance is $43.88. A dental office reminds J. Delgado that it has been 1,095 days since the last cleaning. I pay my own bills online; my own teeth are examined on schedule. Still, I am jealous of J. Delgado’s drama, the way the world knocks on a door that no longer belongs to them. Sometimes I consider paying the $43.88 just to see if gratitude arrives in the form of a corrected statement. I could use a letter that begins “Thank you, J. Delgado,” even if I am only the ghostwriter.

XXI.  
Ghosts are just time tourists with bad itineraries. They show up the hour before your alarm, wearing the face of someone you disappointed. I tell them I have no vacancies, but they never believe me; they drift through the closed door I never installed, trailing the smell of cafeteria tater tots. I have learned to charge them admission: one memory each, payable in exact change. They drop recollections like buttons—plastic disks painted with tiny scenes: a carnival ride stuck mid-spin, a report card folded into a paper airplane, the voicemail icon blinking on a phone now disconnected. I collect them in a jar labeled “Souvenirs from the Before.” When the jar overflows I will dump them into the bathtub and see if they spell a message in the drain.

XXII.  
Drains are the unappreciated oracles of domestic life. They know every hair you shed, every vegetable peel, every bad idea rinsed from a plate. I once watched a spiraling galaxy of cumin seeds disappear down the kitchen sink and felt I was witnessing a tiny apocalypse. The seeds circled faster, a beige Milky Way, then vanished with a gurgle that sounded like a question mark. I leaned over and whispered, “Tell me what I am supposed to do.” The pipes responded with a belch of dish-soap bubbles. Interpretation: keep going.

XXIII.  
Going is easier when you pretend the road is a sentence you are still writing. I drove west last summer until the GPS lady ran out of names and began to stutter. “In 500—500—500 feet, bear—bear—” She sounded like she was freezing to death by subtraction. I turned her off and kept driving into the silence of no signal. The highway became a ribbon of hypothetical grammar: commas of mile markers, semicolons of rest stops, the sudden exclamation point of a deer standing in the median. At dusk I pulled over and opened the windows. The air smelled of iron and irrigation, a scent I recognized only as “not home.” I realized I had driven far enough to lose the accent of humidity, the vowels of crickets I grew up with. In their place, a drier dialect clicked against the windshield like seeds. I wrote three lines in my notebook: “I left to see what would happen if I kept leaving. The answer is more road. The answer is the question wearing a seatbelt.” Then I turned around because the gas gauge blinked like a metronome and I am still afraid of running out, of anything.

XXIV.  
Running out of words feels like standing in a grocery aisle staring at shelves of soup, suddenly unable to recall which variety you have eaten your entire life. The cans blur into a Morse code of red and white. You think, “I came here for something warm,” but the noun escapes, evaporates under fluorescent scrutiny. That is when I resort to gestures: I mime ladling, I blow on an invisible spoon, I shrug so hard my collar touches my ear. Other shoppers steer clear; they have their own missing words, their own evaporations. We are all walking dictionaries with pages torn out by previous owners. Eventually I grab a can at random—tomato rice, a flavor I despise—because at least the act of choosing restores the illusion of agency. At checkout I realize I forgot to bring bags; the clerk offers plastic with such neutrality I cannot tell if it is kindness or indictment. I accept, and the bag becomes a small cloud around my wrist, rustling like an impatient wing.

XXXI.  
Wings are what I wanted when the dentist asked, at age seven, what I would like to be when I grew up. I said “bird,” and he laughed, which I interpreted as encouragement. For months I practiced flapping while running across the backyard, arms strapped with cardboard feathers painted purple. The neighbors’ dog barked in 4/4 time, the sky remained indifferent. One afternoon I tripped on the garden hose and landed nose-first in a rosebush. Thorns performed unexpected acupuncture; blood dotted my cheeks like exclamation points. My mother carried me inside, tweezed the spikes, and told me kindly that humans are earthbound for a reason. I took this as theological doctrine. Years later I learned about hang gliders, parachutes, the entire fraudulent aviation industry, but by then I had already traded flight for metaphor. I became a collector of takeoffs that never leave the ground: the breath you hold before kissing someone for the first time, the elevator lurch in a high-rise, the moment the airplane’s wheels skim the tarmac but have not yet touched. These are flights measured in milliseconds, altitudes of possibility rather than meters. They leave no contrail except the look on your face that strangers later describe as “far away.”

XXXII.  
Far away is where my father lives now, though the phrase really means he died. Funny how English lets distance stand in for absence, as if the dead have simply relocated to a zip code with spotty cell service. I dial his old number sometimes when I know no one will answer, just to hear the disconnected tone that sounds like a small heart flat-lining. The phone company recycled the number; once a teenager answered and I said, “Sorry, wrong universe,” and hung up. I keep his voicemail greeting on an old SIM card in a box with a broken latch. The greeting is only seven seconds: “You’ve reached Don, leave a message.” Short, factual, his voice already thinning with illness. I replay it on anniversaries, on random Tuesdays, whenever the sky looks too big. The paradox of ghosts: they get smaller the longer they stay.

XXXIII.  
Stay is the first word I learned to spell incorrectly. I wrote S-T-A-Y-Y, doubling the final letter the way you double a friend’s hand when you are afraid of crossing the street. My teacher circled the error in red and I felt betrayed by the alphabet itself. Later I understood that doubling is a form of pleading: stay-y, please stay, stay longer than the moment allows. All my misspellings were prophecies. I still add letters when I am tired: happpy, lonng, foreverr. The extra consonants pool on the page like small shadows. Editors circle them; I delete and apologize, but the impulse returns, a stutter in the glove compartment of my intentions.

XXXIV.  
Intentions are maps drawn on napkins, easily shredded by the laundry. I have found them rattling in the dryer like molted snake skin: “Call Maya,” “Learn the difference between lay and lie,” “Forgive yourself for 2004.” They emerge lint-draped, almost unrecognizable, but still legible if you hold them to the light. I iron them flat with my palm and stick them to the fridge with magnets from defunct political campaigns. They curl within hours, returning to the cylinder they long to be. Eventually I let them. Cylinders roll; that is their nature. I cannot blame them for refusing to hang around looking two-dimensional.

XXXV.  
Dimensions collapse in hospital corridors. My friend laid in a room where the clock ran on battery backup, so when the city grid failed during the storm, time itself seemed to drag an extension cord down the hallway, searching for an outlet. We played gin rummy by flashlight; every card dealt was a slapstick shuffle of shadows. She joked that death would arrive wearing mismatched socks because even the afterlife does laundry wrong. I laughed too loudly, the sound bouncing off linoleum like a runaway gurney. When she slept I studied the monitor’s green peaks, each pulse a reluctant mountain. I wanted to ask the mountains what the view was like from up there, but they only beeped. The night nurse told me that beeping is the sound of the body negotiating continuance. I wrote that sentence on the underside of my wrist so I wouldn’t forget, then washed my hands and watched the words spiral into the sink with the soap suds. Some negotiations fail; some sentences are biodegradable.

XXXVI.  
Biodegradable grief surprises you. You expect it to last as long as plastic, to float forever in the ocean of you, choking dolphins. Instead it frays, becomes microscopic, enters the food chain of days until you are made of it at cellular level and no longer notice. One morning you laugh at a meme about otters holding hands and realize the laugh is rounder than before, less edged. The grief has been composted into soil where some new, tender thing is growing—a ridiculous sprout that likes memes, that considers oat milk an acceptable beverage, that maybe applies for a passport. You do not forgive the loss; you just metabolize it. That, too, is resurrection, though it wears everyday clothes.

XXXVII.  
Everyday clothes are what magic wears when it doesn’t want to be spotted on CCTV. I once watched a man pull an entire bouquet of chamomile from the pocket of a windbreaker on the 6 train. He handed each passenger a sprig without explanation. The car smelled like calming tea; strangers tucked stems behind ears, into buttonholes. For six stops we were a mobile herb garden. Then the doors opened at 77th Street and the man stepped off, leaving only the scent and a faint vegetal whisper. I pressed my sprig into my notebook; it left a yellow stain shaped like a tiny country. I never learned its name, but I have been trying to immigrate there ever since.

XXXVIII.  
Since is a slippery preposition; it pretends to measure but really it narrates. “Since the divorce,” “since the pandemic,” “since I last ate grapefruit”—each phrase a doorway you can walk through only one direction. I try to cheat by walking backward, but the rug wrinkles under my heels. Time refuses reverse gear; it will, however, allow pauses. The pause button looks like a porch at dusk, like the moment you notice the neighbor’s cat has different-colored eyes and you kneel to verify the fact. The cat blinks, unimpressed; you have pressed pause, but the cat is still streaming live. This is called shared reality, also known as diplomacy.

XXXIX.  
Diplomacy failed in the produce aisle when I attempted to negotiate a better avocado. I squeezed gently, the way the internet advises, but the avocado replied with a sullen bruise. I returned it to the pyramid, feeling vaguely criminal. A woman beside me whispered, “They’re all like that lately.” Her conspiracy tone implied the avocados were unionized, maybe holding out for better conditions. We exchanged the small shrug of citizens under produce tyranny and selected apples instead. The apples were cordial, democratic, crisp. I left with four and a sense of minor surrender. Later, slicing one, I found the seeds had sprouted inside; tiny white roots reached toward the blade like antennae. I planted them in a yogurt cup. They grew into a small tree that died on a windowsill in Ohio winter. Diplomacy rarely survives the first frost.

XL.  
Frost writes anonymous editorials on the windshield at dawn. I read them with the scrape of plastic, each sweep of the scraper a censoring. The headlines are brief: “Cold Continues,” “Visibility Limited.” I drive to work through censored news, the defroster wheezing like an old accordion. At a red light I notice the car ahead has a bumper sticker: YOU ARE HERE. The arrow points not down to earth but sideways, toward the adjacent lane. I obediently look and see only another driver sipping coffee, steam fogging his glasses. He catches me staring and raises the cup in salute. For a moment the morning feels cooperative, a conspiracy of mutual location. The light changes; we disperse into our separate cartographies. I carry the sticker’s wisdom with me: You are here, but here is always a moving arrow.

XLI.  
Arrows appear in dreams the way parentheses appear in math—grouping, ordering, changing outcome. I dream I am walking through a field of arrows standing on their tails like wheat. They sway, indicating. Choose, they whisper. I pick one and it dissolves into a path of light that ends at the edge of a lake. The surface is glassy, imprinted with a second set of arrows pointing underwater. I wake up before I dive. In the notebook I write: decisions are just arrows that haven’t shot yet. Then I cross out “just,” because minimizing does not protect you from trajectory.

XLII.  
Trajectory is a luxury for people who believe in straight lines. My uncle believed; he mapped his life on graph paper, each square a day to be shaded in with achieved tasks. He died at sixty-one with the final week mostly blank, the pencil slipping from his hand mid-square. At the funeral my cousin placed the graph in the casket, a small white flag surrendering to curvature. I thought about how paper degrades, how the lead will smudge, how the squares will soften into soil. Even rectangles become circles if you wait long enough; the earth is patient potter’s wheel.

XLIII.  
Wheels on shopping carts that squeak are the protest songs of consumerism. I choose the loudest cart on purpose, a mobile demonstration. People turn as I pass the breakfast cereals; I am the town crier announcing, “Oatmeal is still 3 for $5!” The acoustics of the aisle amplify rebellion. By frozen foods I compose a ballad about freezer burn and lost sovereignty. No one applauds, but an elderly man salutes with a bag of peas. In that moment we are comrades, veterans of the same invisible war. Later, in the parking lot, I return the cart to the corral and feel briefly demobilized. The engine turns over; the radio spouts headlines; I re-enlist every mile home.

XLIV.  
Home is the only word that refuses translation. Linguists say it is primal, like “mama,” like “ouch.” I test the theory in airports, eavesdropping on conversations in languages I do not speak. Every tongue circles back to a syllable that sounds like hum, like hmm, like the shape your mouth makes when you taste something almost-forgotten. I say it to myself while fitting the key into my lock after weeks away: home, hmm, home. The apartment exhales stale air, mail piled like small drifts of snow. I walk the perimeter, turning on lights the way some people bless rooms. It is not exciting, this return, but it is the final arrow: You are here.

message.content:
I.  
The first light is always blue. Not the postcard-blue of mid-afternoon sky or the painter’s cobalt squeezed from a tube, but the bruised, almost ultraviolet glow that leaks under doors and around the edges of blackout curtains while the rest of the house still pretends to be asleep. I have been collecting that color for years without knowing what to do with it—scraping it off the rim of coffee cups at 5:13 a.m., catching it in the condensation on the bathroom mirror, folding it into the fitted sheet that snaps like a sail when I finally give up on sleeping. A hundred soft heists. A private indigo archive. I keep it in an imaginary tobacco tin inherited from a grandfather who never smoked, a tin itself inherited from a poem I read in college and immediately misremembered. Sometimes, when the real world’s saturation dial is twisted too high—orange construction vests, red sale banners, the arterial gush of brake lights on the interstate—I unscrew the invisible lid and let the collected blue sigh out like a gas. It doesn’t fix anything, but it lowers the volume.

II.  
There is a kind of loneliness that behaves like a gas too: odorless, colorless, settling first along the floorboards and then rising until it reaches the level of the eyes. You discover it when you come home carrying three plastic bags of groceries in the crook of each arm, kick the door shut behind you, and realize no one else will ever know the exact weight of those bags unless you tell them. The bananas leave tender green bruises on the underside of your wrist; the carton of milk presses its cold square nose against your elbow. You could post a photograph, I suppose—#groceries, #finallyhome—but the weight itself is non-transferable. The loneliness is not the absence of people; it is the absence of witnesses to the microscopic, the almost-nothing, the way the twist-tie from the kale made a tiny helix against your palm. Whole days go by made of almost-nothings. They accumulate like calcium in pipes, a quiet clogging.

III.  
I once tried to keep a diary of almost-nothings. January 9: The rubber band around the asparagus snapped louder than I expected and I laughed out loud, but the laugh came out like a hiccup. February 2: The barista drew a leaf in the foam that looked more like Australia; I drank Australia. February 27: While waiting for the laundry to finish I counted twenty-three dust motes in the shaft of sunlight and lost count when I wondered if “mote” was related to “moat.” I stopped in April because the notebook began to feel like a mausoleum for moments that deserved to evaporate. Still, I cannot shake the urge to testify. Maybe that is what writing is: an inappropriate memorial, a granite bench bolted to the ground beside a butterfly.

IV.  
When I was ten I believed every adult had an internal dashboard with two dials—one labeled Purpose, the other Pleasure—calibrated by some celestial mechanic at age twenty-one. The idea terrified me because I could already sense my own dials wobbling like cheap radio knobs. At ten, Purpose was “paleontologist” one week, “backup dancer for Janet Jackson” the next; Pleasure swung violently between strawberry Pop-Tarts and the breathless seconds before the elementary-school PA speaker crackled out the afternoon announcements. I assumed that someday the needles would settle and lock, the way my parents’ Oldsmobile radio stopped on 101.5 WTAO (“The best of the ’70s, ’80s, and now!”). Then I would know what I was for. I am thirty-eight and the knobs still spin. They spin in my sleep. They spin while I answer emails, while I watch my neighbor across the alley trim his hedges into vague torpedo shapes. Sometimes I imagine prying the plastic bezel off and disconnecting the wires, letting the dials drop behind the console like fallen coins. Quiet. But I am also afraid that if I open the panel I will find nothing back there—just a cavernous dark and the smell of old ozone.

V.  
Science says the universe is expanding at 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec. I looked up “megaparsec” and immediately forgot the definition, the way I forgot the rules of rugby the instant high-school gym ended. Still, the number 70 lingers like a faint bell. I repeat it while doing ordinary things: 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec while folding towels; 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec while scraping burnt rice off the pot. It is a secular prayer, a reminder that the scaffolding is moving, that nothing is clipped to permanence. Sometimes I picture my living room stretching like taffy—couch and coffee table gliding away from each other at imperceptible speed—until the television remote is a continent away and I must mail myself a postcard to recall what I meant to watch. The expansion is impartial; it tugs at blood vessels and shopping lists and the small scar on my knee shaped like a cat’s eye. One day the gap between the scar and the rest of me will be a canyon. I will stand on one rim, shouting “I got it falling off a Schwinn in 1992!” and only an echo shaped like my older sister’s laugh will answer from the far side.

VI.  
My older sister lives in Brisbane, which is 14 hours ahead of Cincinnati. We have performed this arithmetic so long that time zones feel like family lore. When I call her at 7 p.m. my Tuesday, it is 9 a.m. her Wednesday; we meet on the tightrope of that difference. Sometimes she walks her kids to school while I pour a second glass of wine. Her Wednesdays are always shinier than mine, full of sunscreen and morning energy; my Tuesdays carry the sour dusk of a day already threadbare. We trade weather like baseball cards: she sends photos of king parrots on the clothesline, I send icicles forming on the "NO TURN ON RED" sign. The planet keeps tilting, reckless croupier spinning the roulette ball of sunlight. We attempt to steady it with voice memos, with snapshots of cakes we baked or overcooked. The delay on the line is 600 milliseconds—enough for a micro-flinch when our sentences overlap. That half-second contains an entire childhood: the bunk beds, the Glow-Worm nightlight, the cassette deck singing "500 Miles" until the ribbon warped. We let it pass unremarked, the way astronauts let meteors streak past the capsule rather than reroute for every sparkle.

VII.  
Childhood was a series of unauthorized experiments with physics: How long can you lie on the driveway before the gravel embosses your back like braille? How many times can you spin the lawn sprinkler before the hose kinks and water martyrs itself in a burst of mouth-shaped droplets? We tested centrifugal force by sitting on the tire swing, leaning back until the sky inverted and became a blue lake we might dive into headfirst. My sister’s braids collected grass seed; my knees collected ant bites. We recorded data in mud, in cherry Popsicle drips that looked like forensic maps of small empires. No one told us that the rules we were probing were actually statutes written by tired adults: gravity as unpaid intern to liability, thermodynamics as the reason you can’t have seconds of ice cream if you didn’t finish peas. We thought reality was negotiable. In some folder of the cosmos that version of us is still spinning, arms out, ponytails horizontal, believing that if we just went fast enough we could drill a hole in the air and step through to anywhere.

VIII.  
I have never figured out what to do with the moment after arriving anywhere. Airports, hotel rooms, new cities glowing on the horizon like carnival prizes—there is always an awkward minute when the compass needle keeps quivering even though the map says you have arrived. I stand holding my suitcase handle, waiting for some internal gong to announce, “You are here!” but the gong is on back-order. Instead, the ambient soundtrack of the place rushes in: a taxi driver arguing in Tagalog, a child practicing violin through the thin wall of the Airbnb, the sulfuric burp of radiators older than my parents. I open the closet to hang my shirt and find someone else’s dry-cleaning tag still safety-pinned to the wire hanger: R. CHEN, STARCH LIGHT. I imagine R. Chen waking up in the room after me, finding my forgotten dental floss on the nightstand, and feeling briefly, incorrectly, accompanied. We are serial haunters of each other’s unfinished sentences.

IX.  
 unfinished sentences are the only honest kind. Finish a sentence and you have built a wall; leave it open and the weather wanders in. I draft texts I never send: “I dreamed the moon had a concierge desk and your name was on the—“ Backspace backspace. The cursor blinks like a lighthouse that has run out of shipwrecks. Perhaps this is mercy: to spare the other person the burden of replying to a subjunctive moon. Perhaps it is cowardice dressed as mercy, the same way “I’m fine” is a hazmat suit we zip ourselves into before stepping into the contaminated daylight. I read once that the human heart contains a cluster of neurons—40,000 cells lighting up like a galaxy—so maybe when I press delete those little star-cells dim in sequence, a micro-blackout. If you wired me to a heart monitor delicate enough, would it register the vanishing of a clause? Would the EKG spike at the moment I decide not to say I miss you?

X.  
"Miss" is a small word with a trapdoor in the middle. It can mean the distance between two people who once shared everything. I type “I miss—” and delete. The cursor blinks like a lighthouse that has run out of ships to warn.

X.  
The first time I heard the phrase “ontological shock” I was in a university lecture hall at 8 a.m., my brain a marshmallow left too close to the campfire of adolescence. The professor, a man whose mustache looked like it had been drawn on with a fine-tip pen, said it meant the vertigo of realizing that your way of being in the world is just one of many, that the floor is actually a wall somewhere else. I felt it again years later in a Tokyo convenience store at 3 a.m. when I discovered a plastic-wrapped sandwich labeled “Egg Salad with Emergency Rations Flavor.” The neon lights buzzed like hornets; I stood there holding dinner and breakfast and apocalypse in one chilly palm. Ontological shock tastes of refrigerated egg and iodine. It comes in fluorescent packaging, 298 yen, buy-one-get-one during Golden Week.

XI.  
Convenience stores are liminal spaceports. Walk into any 7-Eleven on earth and you have exited local time. The floor is always the same shade of linoleum asteroid; the beep of the register is the universal heartbeat. I have cried in convenience stores on three continents, always next to the rotating grill of skewered sausages. The sausages glisten like minor planets, eternal and indifferent. Once, in Seoul, a clerk wordlessly handed me a packet of tissues while I wept over a mango-flavored yogurt drink. We did not share a language, but we shared the fluorescent hum, the sausages, the protocol that says: here, wipe your face, keep going. I still have the tissue packet; it is printed with cartoon raccoons wearing bow ties. I carry it like a passport stub from a country I hope never to revisit but appreciate having visited.

XII.  
I once tried to write a love letter entirely in questions. The tactic felt safer, as if interrogative grammar could attenuate risk. “Do you remember the sound of the elevator fan in that hotel in Prague? Was it March or November? Could we measure the distance between two heartbeats in floor numbers?” The letter grew to forty-three questions, then forty-four. I mailed it without a return address, a small cowardice dressed as romance. Weeks later I received a postcard: a photograph of the moon over the Atacama Desert. On the back, one sentence, not a question: “Some things answer themselves by echoing.” I have never been to Chile; perhaps the moon has. Perhaps the echo is still ricocheting through the copper mines, looking for a tin roof to land on.

XIII.  
Echoes are the thrift store of memory: everything secondhand, slightly warped, 75 % off original intent. I find them in the alley behind my apartment where the dumpsters reproduce astounding acoustics. If I stand between them at the right angle and say my childhood phone number, it returns to me in the voice of my mother’s kitchen radio, the one with fake wood paneling and a broken tuning dial that always drifted toward the Spanish station. The echo adds a lisp of static, so the eight becomes ocho, the three becomes tres. I am unreachable at that number now; the line was disconnected sometime between the Y2K scare and my father’s second wedding. Still, I recite it monthly, a prayer to the patron saint of discontinued coordinates.

XIV.  
Discontinued things I miss: the glass bottle of milk delivered to the porch, the paper cap you pressed with your thumb to reveal a dab of cream; the sound of VHS tapes rewinding, a mechanical purr that meant the story was being re-coiled, ready to begin again; the smell of library card catalog drawers, sawdust and ink and the faint perfume of every hand that flipped a card forward; the word “post-modern,” which once sounded like a spaceship and now sounds like a dial-up modem. Nostalgia is a museum built inside the mouth; you wander the galleries tasting dust bunnies and Chiclets, trying to remember which decade they belong to.

XV.  
I keep a list of things that refuse to be nostalgic: automated customer service menus, the smell of bus exhaust in August, passwords that must include a capital and a symbol and the blood of a unicorn. These items will never ripen into comfort; they will stay perpetually raw, like the inner cheek you can’t stop biting. The list is written in the notes app of my phone between the grocery list and a half-remembered dream about tidal waves. Every so often I scroll past it and feel the satisfaction of certitude: some hates deserve to be documented, archived, preserved in brine like medical specimens. Future archaeologists will dig up my phone, scroll with delicate tools, and say: “Here was a woman who loathed the phrase ‘your call is important to us.’” They will award me a posthumous medal forged from discontinued earphone jacks.

XVI.  
Medals are just mirrors wearing uniforms. I learned this at a middle-school awards ceremony where I received a participation ribbon for “Most Improved in Volleyball.” I was allergic to the metallic thread; within minutes my chest erupted in hives shaped like tiny crowns. While the nurse dabbed calamine on my sternum, I understood that validation can be histamine. Since then I have distrusted any accolade that must be pinned to fabric. I prefer applause that disperses immediately, the kind children give when they’ve already turned their backs to run toward the next game. Ephemeral applause is honest; it knows it is leaving.

XVII.  
Leaving is a skill I never mastered. I stand in doorways too long, one foot on the welcome mat, one foot in the future, my tongue stacking goodbyes like a malfunctioning vending machine. The problem is that every threshold looks like a question mark lying on its side, inviting me to drag it upright and punctuate something. Instead I mumble “see you later” to people I will never see again, consigning the sentence to a lifetime of logistical confusion. I once spent twenty minutes saying goodbye to a therapist after our final session; she finally walked me out, patted my shoulder, and said, “It’s okay, endings are just beginnings that have taken off their hat.” I still wonder what kind of hat—top hat, sunbonnet, maybe a fascinator decked with miniature beginnings. I picture them lined up on a shelf in her office, hats looking for heads, heads looking for doors.

XVIII.  
Doors are books with hinges. Open one and you read a new temperature on your skin, a new dialect of creak. I collect doorknobs in a drawer—porcelain, brass, glass like a witch’s crystal ball. When I can’t sleep I hold them and try to intuit which room they once guarded. The oval brass one feels like a hotel in a city where I arrived drenched from a sudden storm; the porcelain one carries a faint odor of rosewater and old tax documents. I rotate them like worry beads, hoping for a transmission, a clue to the topography on the other side of time. Occasionally I imagine screwing one into the wall beside my bed, a door to nowhere, just to have the option. If I ever install it, I will paint “Elsewhere” on the jamb and charge admission: one secret per entry, payable in expired transit tokens.

XIX.  
Tokens are fossils of future archaeology. The subway tokens in my change jar will outlast the system they once animated, just as the brass tokens my grandmother used at the textile mill now lie in my palm like miniature suns. I like to dump them on the table and try to read them like runes. One says: you will be delayed. Another says: transfer available. The smallest, smoothest one says nothing, and that seems the most honest fortune of all. I carry it in my coat pocket as a reminder that silence is a valid currency; sometimes you pay with not-speaking, sometimes you receive.

XX.  
Receiving mail addressed to the previous tenant feels like intercepting a postcard from an alternate universe. I pile the envelopes on the kitchen counter, a small shrine to J. Delgado, who once inhabited these rooms before I did. The electric company wants J. Delgado to know the overdue balance is $43.88. A dental office reminds J. Delgado that it has been 1,095 days since the last cleaning. I pay my own bills online; my own teeth are examined on schedule. Still, I am jealous of J. Delgado’s drama, the way the world knocks on a door that no longer belongs to them. Sometimes I consider paying the $43.88 just to see if gratitude arrives in the form of a corrected statement. I could use a letter that begins “Thank you, J. Delgado,” even if I am only the ghostwriter.

XXI.  
Ghosts are just time tourists with bad itineraries. They show up the hour before your alarm, wearing the face of someone you disappointed. I tell them I have no vacancies, but they never believe me; they drift through the closed door I never installed, trailing the smell of cafeteria tater tots. I have learned to charge them admission: one memory each, payable in exact change. They drop recollections like buttons—plastic disks painted with tiny scenes: a carnival ride stuck mid-spin, a report card folded into a paper airplane, the voicemail icon blinking on a phone now disconnected. I collect them in a jar labeled “Souvenirs from the Before.” When the jar overflows I will dump them into the bathtub and see if they spell a message in the drain.

XXII.  
Drains are the unappreciated oracles of domestic life. They know every hair you shed, every vegetable peel, every bad idea rinsed from a plate. I once watched a spiraling galaxy of cumin seeds disappear down the kitchen sink and felt I was witnessing a tiny apocalypse. The seeds circled faster, a beige Milky Way, then vanished with a gurgle that sounded like a question mark. I leaned over and whispered, “Tell me what I am supposed to do.” The pipes responded with a belch of dish-soap bubbles. Interpretation: keep going.

XXIII.  
Going is easier when you pretend the road is a sentence you are still writing. I drove west last summer until the GPS lady ran out of names and began to stutter. “In 500—500—500 feet, bear—bear—” She sounded like she was freezing to death by subtraction. I turned her off and kept driving into the silence of no signal. The highway became a ribbon of hypothetical grammar: commas of mile markers, semicolons of rest stops, the sudden exclamation point of a deer standing in the median. At dusk I pulled over and opened the windows. The air smelled of iron and irrigation, a scent I recognized only as “not home.” I realized I had driven far enough to lose the accent of humidity, the vowels of crickets I grew up with. In their place, a drier dialect clicked against the windshield like seeds. I wrote three lines in my notebook: “I left to see what would happen if I kept leaving. The answer is more road. The answer is the question wearing a seatbelt.” Then I turned around because the gas gauge blinked like a metronome and I am still afraid of running out, of anything.

XXIV.  
Running out of words feels like standing in a grocery aisle staring at shelves of soup, suddenly unable to recall which variety you have eaten your entire life. The cans blur into a Morse code of red and white. You think, “I came here for something warm,” but the noun escapes, evaporates under fluorescent scrutiny. That is when I resort to gestures: I mime ladling, I blow on an invisible spoon, I shrug so hard my collar touches my ear. Other shoppers steer clear; they have their own missing words, their own evaporations. We are all walking dictionaries with pages torn out by previous owners. Eventually I grab a can at random—tomato rice, a flavor I despise—because at least the act of choosing restores the illusion of agency. At checkout I realize I forgot to bring bags; the clerk offers plastic with such neutrality I cannot tell if it is kindness or indictment. I accept, and the bag becomes a small cloud around my wrist, rustling like an impatient wing.

XXXI.  
Wings are what I wanted when the dentist asked, at age seven, what I would like to be when I grew up. I said “bird,” and he laughed, which I interpreted as encouragement. For months I practiced flapping while running across the backyard, arms strapped with cardboard feathers painted purple. The neighbors’ dog barked in 4/4 time, the sky remained indifferent. One afternoon I tripped on the garden hose and landed nose-first in a rosebush. Thorns performed unexpected acupuncture; blood dotted my cheeks like exclamation points. My mother carried me inside, tweezed the spikes, and told me kindly that humans are earthbound for a reason. I took this as theological doctrine. Years later I learned about hang gliders, parachutes, the entire fraudulent aviation industry, but by then I had already traded flight for metaphor. I became a collector of takeoffs that never leave the ground: the breath you hold before kissing someone for the first time, the elevator lurch in a high-rise, the moment the airplane’s wheels skim the tarmac but have not yet touched. These are flights measured in milliseconds, altitudes of possibility rather than meters. They leave no contrail except the look on your face that strangers later describe as “far away.”

XXXII.  
Far away is where my father lives now, though the phrase really means he died. Funny how English lets distance stand in for absence, as if the dead have simply relocated to a zip code with spotty cell service. I dial his old number sometimes when I know no one will answer, just to hear the disconnected tone that sounds like a small heart flat-lining. The phone company recycled the number; once a teenager answered and I said, “Sorry, wrong universe,” and hung up. I keep his voicemail greeting on an old SIM card in a box with a broken latch. The greeting is only seven seconds: “You’ve reached Don, leave a message.” Short, factual, his voice already thinning with illness. I replay it on anniversaries, on random Tuesdays, whenever the sky looks too big. The paradox of ghosts: they get smaller the longer they stay.

XXXIII.  
Stay is the first word I learned to spell incorrectly. I wrote S-T-A-Y-Y, doubling the final letter the way you double a friend’s hand when you are afraid of crossing the street. My teacher circled the error in red and I felt betrayed by the alphabet itself. Later I understood that doubling is a form of pleading: stay-y, please stay, stay longer than the moment allows. All my misspellings were prophecies. I still add letters when I am tired: happpy, lonng, foreverr. The extra consonants pool on the page like small shadows. Editors circle them; I delete and apologize, but the impulse returns, a stutter in the glove compartment of my intentions.

XXXIV.  
Intentions are maps drawn on napkins, easily shredded by the laundry. I have found them rattling in the dryer like molted snake skin: “Call Maya,” “Learn the difference between lay and lie,” “Forgive yourself for 2004.” They emerge lint-draped, almost unrecognizable, but still legible if you hold them to the light. I iron them flat with my palm and stick them to the fridge with magnets from defunct political campaigns. They curl within hours, returning to the cylinder they long to be. Eventually I let them. Cylinders roll; that is their nature. I cannot blame them for refusing to hang around looking two-dimensional.

XXXV.  
Dimensions collapse in hospital corridors. My friend laid in a room where the clock ran on battery backup, so when the city grid failed during the storm, time itself seemed to drag an extension cord down the hallway, searching for an outlet. We played gin rummy by flashlight; every card dealt was a slapstick shuffle of shadows. She joked that death would arrive wearing mismatched socks because even the afterlife does laundry wrong. I laughed too loudly, the sound bouncing off linoleum like a runaway gurney. When she slept I studied the monitor’s green peaks, each pulse a reluctant mountain. I wanted to ask the mountains what the view was like from up there, but they only beeped. The night nurse told me that beeping is the sound of the body negotiating continuance. I wrote that sentence on the underside of my wrist so I wouldn’t forget, then washed my hands and watched the words spiral into the sink with the soap suds. Some negotiations fail; some sentences are biodegradable.

XXXVI.  
Biodegradable grief surprises you. You expect it to last as long as plastic, to float forever in the ocean of you, choking dolphins. Instead it frays, becomes microscopic, enters the food chain of days until you are made of it at cellular level and no longer notice. One morning you laugh at a meme about otters holding hands and realize the laugh is rounder than before, less edged. The grief has been composted into soil where some new, tender thing is growing—a ridiculous sprout that likes memes, that considers oat milk an acceptable beverage, that maybe applies for a passport. You do not forgive the loss; you just metabolize it. That, too, is resurrection, though it wears everyday clothes.

XXXVII.  
Everyday clothes are what magic wears when it doesn’t want to be spotted on CCTV. I once watched a man pull an entire bouquet of chamomile from the pocket of a windbreaker on the 6 train. He handed each passenger a sprig without explanation. The car smelled like calming tea; strangers tucked stems behind ears, into buttonholes. For six stops we were a mobile herb garden. Then the doors opened at 77th Street and the man stepped off, leaving only the scent and a faint vegetal whisper. I pressed my sprig into my notebook; it left a yellow stain shaped like a tiny country. I never learned its name, but I have been trying to immigrate there ever since.

XXXVIII.  
Since is a slippery preposition; it pretends to measure but really it narrates. “Since the divorce,” “since the pandemic,” “since I last ate grapefruit”—each phrase a doorway you can walk through only one direction. I try to cheat by walking backward, but the rug wrinkles under my heels. Time refuses reverse gear; it will, however, allow pauses. The pause button looks like a porch at dusk, like the moment you notice the neighbor’s cat has different-colored eyes and you kneel to verify the fact. The cat blinks, unimpressed; you have pressed pause, but the cat is still streaming live. This is called shared reality, also known as diplomacy.

XXXIX.  
Diplomacy failed in the produce aisle when I attempted to negotiate a better avocado. I squeezed gently, the way the internet advises, but the avocado replied with a sullen bruise. I returned it to the pyramid, feeling vaguely criminal. A woman beside me whispered, “They’re all like that lately.” Her conspiracy tone implied the avocados were unionized, maybe holding out for better conditions. We exchanged the small shrug of citizens under produce tyranny and selected apples instead. The apples were cordial, democratic, crisp. I left with four and a sense of minor surrender. Later, slicing one, I found the seeds had sprouted inside; tiny white roots reached toward the blade like antennae. I planted them in a yogurt cup. They grew into a small tree that died on a windowsill in Ohio winter. Diplomacy rarely survives the first frost.

XL.  
Frost writes anonymous editorials on the windshield at dawn. I read them with the scrape of plastic, each sweep of the scraper a censoring. The headlines are brief: “Cold Continues,” “Visibility Limited.” I drive to work through censored news, the defroster wheezing like an old accordion. At a red light I notice the car ahead has a bumper sticker: YOU ARE HERE. The arrow points not down to earth but sideways, toward the adjacent lane. I obediently look and see only another driver sipping coffee, steam fogging his glasses. He catches me staring and raises the cup in salute. For a moment the morning feels cooperative, a conspiracy of mutual location. The light changes; we disperse into our separate cartographies. I carry the sticker’s wisdom with me: You are here, but here is always a moving arrow.

XLI.  
Arrows appear in dreams the way parentheses appear in math—grouping, ordering, changing outcome. I dream I am walking through a field of arrows standing on their tails like wheat. They sway, indicating. Choose, they whisper. I pick one and it dissolves into a path of light that ends at the edge of a lake. The surface is glassy, imprinted with a second set of arrows pointing underwater. I wake up before I dive. In the notebook I write: decisions are just arrows that haven’t shot yet. Then I cross out “just,” because minimizing does not protect you from trajectory.

XLII.  
Trajectory is a luxury for people who believe in straight lines. My uncle believed; he mapped his life on graph paper, each square a day to be shaded in with achieved tasks. He died at sixty-one with the final week mostly blank, the pencil slipping from his hand mid-square. At the funeral my cousin placed the graph in the casket, a small white flag surrendering to curvature. I thought about how paper degrades, how the lead will smudge, how the squares will soften into soil. Even rectangles become circles if you wait long enough; the earth is patient potter’s wheel.

XLIII.  
Wheels on shopping carts that squeak are the protest songs of consumerism. I choose the loudest cart on purpose, a mobile demonstration. People turn as I pass the breakfast cereals; I am the town crier announcing, “Oatmeal is still 3 for $5!” The acoustics of the aisle amplify rebellion. By frozen foods I compose a ballad about freezer burn and lost sovereignty. No one applauds, but an elderly man salutes with a bag of peas. In that moment we are comrades, veterans of the same invisible war. Later, in the parking lot, I return the cart to the corral and feel briefly demobilized. The engine turns over; the radio spouts headlines; I re-enlist every mile home.

XLIV.  
Home is the only word that refuses translation. Linguists say it is primal, like “mama,” like “ouch.” I test the theory in airports, eavesdropping on conversations in languages I do not speak. Every tongue circles back to a syllable that sounds like hum, like hmm, like the shape your mouth makes when you taste something almost-forgotten. I say it to myself while fitting the key into my lock after weeks away: home, hmm, home. The apartment exhales stale air, mail piled like small drifts of snow. I walk the perimeter, turning on lights the way some people bless rooms. It is not exciting, this return, but it is the final arrow: You are here.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
